author: Vitor
aiExplanation: This is a **sliding window** approach that counts substrings containing at least one `'a'`, `'b'`, and `'c'`. It uses a `dp` array of 26 slots (one per lowercase letter) to track character frequencies, expanding a `right` pointer across the string. Whenever `dp[0]`, `dp[1]`, and `dp[2]` (representing `'a'`, `'b'`, `'c'`) are all greater than zero, every extension of the current window to the right also satisfies the condition, so it adds `n - right` to the result. Then it **shrinks the window from the left** by decrementing the count of `s[left]` and advancing `left`, repeating until the condition no longer holds.

author: Vitor
aiExplanation: This is the same **sliding window** algorithm but uses three individual counters (`a`, `b`, `c`) instead of an array. It expands the `right` pointer, incrementing the appropriate counter based on the current character using `if/else if` checks. When all three counters are positive, the window is valid and every substring extending further right is also valid, so it adds `n - right` to the result; it then **contracts from the left**, decrementing the relevant counter until the window is no longer valid.

author: Vitor
aiExplanation: This is the same **sliding window** algorithm as the previous solutions, but uses `switch` statements instead of `if/else if` chains for character matching. It maintains three counters (`a`, `b`, `c`) and a `left` pointer. As the `right` pointer advances, it increments the matching counter; once all three are non-zero, it adds `s.length - i` to the result (counting all valid extensions to the right) and then **shrinks the left boundary** via another `switch` until the window no longer contains all three characters.
